metadata.summary: Enforce Go layout dependency direction: handler imports service interface, service imports repository interface, repository implements it. Depguard + golangci-lint as the automated gate.

content.title: Go Standard Layout — Layer Dependency Rules
content.summary: The Go standard layout only enforces package visibility via internal/ — it does NOT enforce dependency direction between layers. Without depguard or equivalent import linting, handler packages freely import repository packages directly, bypassing the service layer. Explicit rules + automated linting are required to make the layout load-bearing.

why.p: Layered packages do not enforce dependency direction by themselves. Go's compiler will happily compile internal/handler importing internal/repository, bypassing internal/service entirely. Without depguard or import linting, the layout is decorative — it looks like Clean Architecture but does not prevent the shortcut imports that accumulate into a big ball of mud.

Interface placement is the second common failure: when interfaces are declared in the producer package (e.g., repository.UserRepository in internal/repository), every consumer must import the producer, creating fan-in coupling. The correct Go pattern is interfaces at the consumer side — service.UserRepository lives in internal/service and internal/repository implements it — enabling dependency inversion without import cycles.

list.item: Any Go service following the standard layout where you want the layer seams to be mechanically enforced, not just conventional.
Team or multi-agent settings where different authors might shortcut handler-to-repository imports.
Services large enough that a single violated import boundary significantly increases coupling.
CI pipelines that must gate on architecture violations the way they gate on test failures.
Tiny CLIs or scripts — the overhead of a linter config and interface-at-consumer discipline exceeds the value.
Services already using a different architecture (hexagonal, package-by-feature) — applying these rules on top of a different model causes confusion.

rule.statement: Interfaces MUST be defined at the consumer side. service.UserRepository lives in internal/service; internal/repository implements it. Handler interfaces (if any) live in internal/handler. Never declare an interface in its own implementation package.
rule.rationale: Consumer-side interfaces enable dependency inversion: the service layer owns the contract; the repository layer provides the implementation. This prevents import cycles and allows repository implementations to be replaced without touching consumers.

rule.statement: handler MUST NOT import repository directly. The allowed import chain is: handler imports service interface; service imports repository interface; repository implementation imports model and DB driver. Enforce via depguard in .golangci.yml.
rule.rationale: Skipping the service layer in a handler couples HTTP concerns directly to persistence, making it impossible to test the handler with a mock repository or the service with a mock DB without spinning up the full stack.

rule.statement: service MUST NOT import the web framework (e.g., gin-gonic/gin). Service methods receive context.Context and domain types only.
rule.rationale: A service that imports gin is untestable without an HTTP server. Keeping framework types at the handler boundary makes services trivially testable with table-driven unit tests.

rule.statement: model (domain types) MUST NOT import database/sql or any ORM. The translation from DB rows to domain types happens in the repository implementation, not in the model package.
rule.rationale: Domain types that import DB drivers couple the domain model to infrastructure, preventing reuse across different storage backends and making the model hard to test in isolation.

rule.statement: Every service and repository method signature MUST take ctx context.Context as the first parameter. Errors crossing a layer boundary MUST be wrapped with fmt.Errorf("...: %w", err).
rule.rationale: Context-first ensures cancellation propagates; %w-wrapped errors preserve the error chain so errors.Is and errors.As work across layers and in log aggregators.

rule.statement: Repository implementations MUST return domain models, not ORM rows or raw sql.Rows. The translation happens inside the repository impl.
rule.rationale: Service and handler layers that receive domain types instead of ORM objects are decoupled from the choice of persistence library.

rule.statement: Global state (var DB *sql.DB, var logger *zerolog.Logger) MUST NOT appear at package scope in any package other than cmd/name/main.go. Use constructor injection from the entry point.
rule.rationale: Package-global state makes test isolation impossible without os.Setenv hacks or singleton resets; constructor injection from main makes every dependency explicit and replaceable.

rule.statement: init() functions MUST NOT register handlers, apply migrations, or perform I/O. Use explicit constructors called from main.
rule.rationale: init() side effects execute during import, making test-suite startup order brittle and preventing targeted disabling of components in integration tests.

rule.statement: One linter config per repo. Commit .golangci.yml; CI runs golangci-lint run --timeout=3m on every PR. Do not rely on per-developer local setup.
rule.rationale: Without a committed linter config, lint rules drift per developer and per CI run; the config file is the authoritative record of what the team agreed to enforce.

section.name: depguard-config
section.p: Add a .golangci.yml to the repo root enforcing the layer dependency rules. The depguard section forbids the critical shortcut imports:

example.title: .golangci.yml with depguard layer rules
example.code: linters: enable: [depguard, errcheck, govet, staticcheck, revive, gocyclo, gosec] linters-settings: depguard: rules: handler: files: ["internal/handler/**"] deny: - pkg: "project/internal/repository" desc: "handler must call service, not repository" service: files: ["internal/service/**"] deny: - pkg: "github.com/gin-gonic/gin" desc: "service must not depend on web framework" domain: files: ["internal/model/**"] deny: - pkg: "database/sql" desc: "model must not depend on DB driver"
example.note: Replace "project" with your actual module path from go.mod.

section.name: layout-lint-script
section.p: A lightweight shell script for fast local layout sanity checks, independent of golangci-lint. Run it as a pre-push hook or in CI alongside the linter:

example.title: go-layout-lint.sh — quick import sanity checks
example.code: #!/usr/bin/env bash # go-layout-lint.sh — quick layout sanity checks set -euo pipefail root="${1:-.}" fail=0 echo "## handler importing repository directly" grep -rEn '"[^"]+/internal/repository"' "$root/internal/handler" 2>/dev/null && fail=1 || true echo "## interface in producer (repo) package" for f in "$root"/internal/repository/*.go; do grep -Eq '^type [A-Z][a-zA-Z]+ interface' "$f" && { echo "iface in producer: $f"; fail=1; } done echo "## pkg/ with no consumer outside repo" [[ -d "$root/pkg" ]] && for d in "$root"/pkg/*/; do name=$(basename "$d") grep -rEq "\"[^\"]+/pkg/$name\"" "$root" --exclude-dir="pkg" || { echo "unused pkg/$name"; fail=1; } done exit "$fail"
example.note: Pass the repo root as the first argument: bash go-layout-lint.sh ./myservice. Exits 1 on any violation.

section.name: test-placement
section.p: Test co-location and black-box testing convention:

rule.statement: Tests MUST be placed next to the code they test (*_test.go co-located). For black-box style, use package handler_test (external test package) to avoid accessing unexported internals. Use testcontainers-go for repository tests requiring real Postgres/Redis.
rule.rationale: Black-box tests prevent tests from becoming coupled to internal implementation details; testcontainers-go gives repository tests a real DB without mocks, catching SQL dialect issues that in-memory fakes miss.

example.title: Testing on the wrong layer
example.note: Force handler tests with mocked services; force repository tests with testcontainers-go. Never wire the full stack in a handler test.
